data.title: BP Chair Manifold re-elected but two board proposals rejected at AGM
data.description:
BP (BP) Chair Albert Manifold was re-elected Thursday with 81.8% of the vote at his first annual general meeting, but investors rejected two of the board's proposals, showing frustration over the company's approach to climate disclosures and corporate governance.

The company had offered two special resolutions that each gained just 47% of voting investors, far below the 75% threshold required for the proposals to pass.

BP (BP) had asked for permission to revoke two previous shareholder resolutions from 2015 and 2019 that required the release of climate-related data, which the company said had been made redundant thanks to mandatory climate disclosures.

The board also submitted a resolution to allow electronic-only general meetings, which it said would allow more of its shareholders outside the U.K. to participate.

More than 25% of shareholders backed a shareholder resolution calling on BP (BP) to justifyits capital expenditure on upstream oil and gas, which was filed by the Australasian Centre forCorporate Responsibility.

Manifold, who became Chair in October, is overseeing a strategic reset and a return to fossil-fuel exploration and production to boost profitability; advisory group Glass Lewis had recommended that investors vote against him over concerns about governance, but Norway's sovereign wealth fund—BP's largest shareholder—came out in support.

The buildup ahead of the meeting was marked by criticism about some of BP's (BP) resolutions, and the omission of a resolution from climate activist group Follow This led to complaints over the erosion of shareholder rights.

data.title: Bankwell forecasts $12M-$13M in 2026 noninterest income while reaffirming $111M-$112M net interest income
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bankwell Financial Group (BWFG) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We've delivered a solid start to 2026 with strong earnings, continued balance sheet improvement and continued progress on our strategic priorities," said (CEO & Director Christopher Gruseke), adding, "For the first quarter, we reported GAAP net income of $11.3 million or $1.41 per share."

  • (CEO Gruseke) tied results to balance-sheet actions and mix shift, saying results were supported by "solid loan production, strong fee income from our SBA platform, lower funding costs, meaningful core deposit growth and ongoing balance sheet optimization, including reduced reliance on wholesale funding and continued progress on building a more interest rate neutral balance sheet."

  • (CEO Gruseke) highlighted production and funding, saying, "Loan growth remained positive during the quarter with $190 million of originations, including $34 million of SBA production, resulting in net loan growth of $27 million," and added, "Core deposits increased by $113 million sequentially with $39 million coming from low-cost deposits."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Courtney Sacchetti) described margin dynamics and deposit repricing, stating, "Net interest income totaled $26.9 million for the first quarter and was largely unchanged compared to the prior quarter," and "Total deposit costs declined to 310 basis points, down 5 basis points from the fourth quarter, and the bank exited March with a deposit cost exit rate of approximately 298 basis points."

  • (CFO Sacchetti) quantified deposit repricing embedded in coming periods, saying, "During the first quarter, we successfully repriced approximately $300 million of time deposits, 44 basis points lower, generating an expected annualized benefit of $1.2 million," and "over the next 12 months, approximately $1.1 billion of time deposits are expected to reprice favorably with an average rate reduction of 14 basis points."

  • (President & Chief Banking Officer Matthew McNeill) framed the new New York branch as deposit-led, saying, "It wasn't the primary reason to open the office. It was definitely a deposit play, which has already taken off and been robust," while noting, "Lending isn't a part of the strategy there."

Outlook

  • (CFO Sacchetti) reaffirmed the full-year net interest income range, stating, "As deposit repricing continues to flow through the balance sheet and interest rate sensitivity moderates, we expect incremental margin improvement over the balance of 2026, affirming our full year net interest income guidance of $111 million to $112 million."

  • (CFO Sacchetti) raised the full-year noninterest income range, stating, "Based on our first quarter results, we are raising our full year noninterest income guidance to $12 million to $13 million."

  • (CEO Gruseke) reiterated loan growth pacing versus prior guidance, saying, "On an annualized basis, this level of growth is consistent with our previously communicated guidance of 4% to 5% for the full year, and our pipeline remains strong."

  • Guidance language vs. last quarter emphasized fee-income upside: Q4 2025 guidance cited "noninterest income to increase to approximately $11 million to $12 million," while Q1 2026 updated it to "$12 million to $13 million" (both per (CFO Sacchetti)).

Financial results

  • (CFO Sacchetti) reported profitability and expense seasonality, stating, "Profitability remained solid in the quarter with return on average assets of 1.35% and a return on average tangible common equity of 15%," while also noting, "Our PPNR was impacted by approximately $1 million in annual noninterest expense typically incurred in the first quarter, elevating total noninterest expense to $16.9 million for the quarter."

  • (CFO Sacchetti) detailed fee mix and SBA contribution, saying, "Noninterest income totaled $3.3 million for the quarter, reflecting $2.4 million of gains on SBA loan sales," and added the expense outlook was unchanged: "our underlying noninterest expense run rate remains consistent with our prior guidance of $64 million to $65 million."

  • (CFO Sacchetti) summarized reserve and capital actions, stating, "Our provision for credit losses was a release of $1 million for the quarter," and "We repurchased 3,317 shares during the quarter at an average price of $45.32 per share."

Q&A

  • Mark Shutley, KBW: asked about deposit competition and the ability to lower deposit costs; (President McNeill) responded, "it's very competitive out there for deposits" and added, "we're finding success and have been able to substantially grow core deposits in the quarter," while (CEO Gruseke) emphasized management’s approach: "we're happy with the deposit result despite the competitive environment."

  • Mark Shutley, KBW: asked whether SBA originations assumptions were changing and how SBA feeds fee guidance; (President McNeill) said, "We could definitely originate more SBA loans" but added, "We're choosing to keep the volume kind of level where it's at. We're not increasing our $100 million," and (CEO Gruseke) reinforced, "So if we wanted to do more, we could is the answer."

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde: asked about the Brooklyn office strategy and expected lending; (President McNeill) answered, "It wasn't the primary reason to open the office. It was definitely a deposit play," and "I think we'll do a modest amount of lending out of the office."

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde: asked if CRE concentration could fall below 300% and whether management targets that threshold; (CEO Gruseke) replied, "We don't have 300 as a target," adding, "It's conceivable, but it's not the plan" and "I wouldn't be surprised if it came down another 10, 20 basis points over the course of the year."

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde: asked about the increase in nonaccruals and resolution; (President McNeill) said, "The increase was just a tenant left the building," and added, "We think that we'll be able to work with them to dispose of the real estate and be paid there."

  • Chase Steward, Raymond James: asked about new origination yields; (CFO Sacchetti) responded, "For the first quarter, our average rate was 7.5%." Chase Steward, Raymond James: asked what would drive more buybacks; (CEO Gruseke) answered, "you have to look at our consolidated CET1 ratio" and added, "our goal is still to get to 11%, not necessarily by year-end."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly negative, pressing on deposit competition, CRE concentration, nonaccrual drivers, and capital return; questions such as "Are you seeing more competition on the deposit side?" (Mark Shutley, KBW) and "can you speak a little bit more on maybe what drove the increase" (Feddie Strickland, Hovde) focused on funding and credit.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and pragmatic in Q&A, leaning on execution and specific levers; examples included "we expect incremental margin improvement" (Executive VP & CFO Sacchetti) and "it's very competitive out there for deposits" (President McNeill).

  • Versus Q4 2025, management messaging added a clear uplift in fee-income guidance ("raising our full year noninterest income guidance") while Q&A remained centered on similar themes (deposit mix, SBA durability, loan mix/CRE, and capital ratios).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Profit drivers shifted modestly from Q4’s emphasis on margin expansion ("Net interest margin expanded to 340 basis points" in Q4) to Q1’s emphasis on managing NIM pressure ("modest pressure from asset repricing" and "unfavorable day count impact" per (CEO Gruseke) and (CFO Sacchetti)).

  • Guidance changed primarily in fee outlook: Q4 guided "noninterest income... approximately $11 million to $12 million" (CEO Gruseke), while Q1 raised to "$12 million to $13 million" (CFO Sacchetti), with net interest income guidance reiterated at "$111 million to $112 million" (CEO Gruseke in Q4; (CFO Sacchetti) in Q1).

  • Analysts’ focus stayed consistent on deposits and SBA; Q1 added more scrutiny on Brooklyn strategy and explicit probing on buybacks and the CET1 goal.

Risks and concerns

  • (President McNeill) flagged deposit-market intensity: "it's very competitive out there for deposits," with management emphasizing mix shift and "bringing in low-cost deposits" as the mitigation.

  • (CFO Sacchetti) described margin headwinds from rate dynamics, citing "the repricing of floating rate loans in a lower rate environment" and the day-count impact, while pointing to time-deposit repricing benefits as an offset.

  • Credit risk discussion centered on specific CRE stress tied to occupancy: "a tenant left the building" and "Sponsors not able to make the payment" (President McNeill), with mitigation framed as workout and sale: "dispose of the real estate and be paid there."

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a strong earnings start supported by core deposit growth, reduced reliance on wholesale funding, and SBA-driven fee income. The company reaffirmed 2026 net interest income guidance of $111 million to $112 million, raised noninterest income guidance to $12 million to $13 million, and reiterated loan growth pacing consistent with its 4% to 5% full-year target. In Q&A, analysts pressed on deposit competition, CRE concentration, and nonaccrual drivers, while management emphasized deposit-led expansion in Brooklyn, visibility into credit resolutions, and a continued focus on building the CET1 ratio toward an 11% goal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: STMicroelectronics surges after Q1 revenue soars, Q2 sales outlook beats estimates
data.description:
Shares of STMicroelectronics (STM) jumped about 12% on Thursday after the second quarter revenue outlook surpassed estimates.

Q1 Metrics

STMicroelectronics — in which the French and Italian governments hold a stake — saw net revenues for the first quarter ended March 28 surge 23% year-over-year to $3.1B, beating estimates. Meanwhile, non-U.S. GAAP EPS soared 85.7% year-over-year to $0.13 but missed analysts' expectations.

"In the first quarter, despite the macroeconomic uncertainty, we saw improving demand with strong booking and normalized inventory in distribution," said STMicroelectronics' President and CEO Jean-Marc Chery in a CEO script posted by the company.

"Q1 net revenues, excluding the contribution of our acquisition of NXP’s MEMS sensor business, came above the mid-point of our business outlook range, driven mainly by higher revenues in our engaged customer programs in Personal electronics and CECP," Chery noted in the earnings press release.

STMicroelectronics noted that in the first quarter it reported a $70M operating income, which included $71M for impairment, restructuring charges, and other related phase-out costs. These charges were related to the execution of the previously announced company-wide program to reshape its manufacturing footprint and resize its global cost base. The operating income also included $30M Purchase Price Allocation, or PPA, effects from the company's acquisition of NXP’s MEMS sensor business. Excluding these items, non-U.S. GAAP operating income stood at $171M in the first quarter.

Analog products, MEMS and Sensors (AM&S) segment revenue grew 23.2% year-over-year to $1.32B, mainly due to Imaging and MEMS and, to a lesser extent, Analog, according to the company.

Revenue from the Power and Discrete products (P&D) segment decreased 1.8% year-over-year to $389M. Meanwhile, revenue from the Microcontrollers, Digital ICs and RF products (MDRF) Product Group jumped 32.1% year-over-year to $1.38B.

In the first quarter, the company paid cash dividends totaling $71M to its stockholders.

Outlook

"In the second quarter we expect revenues well above average seasonality, as well as an increased gross margin. We have a clear path to improve gross margin while staying at the forefront of innovation. We expect 2026 revenues to show double-digit growth. Beyond our addressable market dynamics and our already engaged customer programs, this growth will be driven by new AI programs, for which we leverage our specialized technologies to enable the evolving AI infrastructure," said Chery.

STMicroelectronics said net revenues for the second quarter are expected to be $3.45B, an increase of 11.6% sequentially, plus or minus 350 basis points. The consensus revenue estimate is $3.19B.

The company expects a U.S. GAAP gross margin of 34.8%, plus or minus 200 basis points. Non-U.S. GAAP gross margin is expected to be about 35.2%, plus or minus 200 basis points.

"ST is now strategically positioned to capture upside from new AI driven programs, leveraging specialized technologies to enable the evolving AI infrastructure, confirming our datacenters revenue expectation to be nicely above $500 million for 2026 and well above $1 billion for 2027," said Chery.

Chery noted in the CEO script that on May 4, the company will host a dedicated call on LEO satellites, explaining how it is going to achieve its ambition of "well above $3 billion cumulative revenues over the period 2026-2028 for this opportunity."

Wall Street Analysts

Jefferies kept its Buy rating on the stock with a €35 price target.

Analysts led by Janardan Menon said that the upside to revenues seems to have come fromcontinued strength at Apple (AAPL), data centers, LEO-related systems, and the acquisition of NXP's MEMS sensor business.

"We estimate that most of this data center-related growth is coming from making components for optical connectivity for AWS, Innolight and other customers. We believe the company is at the early stages of a new upcycle, with further upgrades to estimates likely in future quarters," said Menon and his team.

Related stocks: Texas Instruments (TXN) surged about 17% after reporting its first quarter 2026 financial results, while NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) soared around 7%. Analog Devices (ADI) jumped about 6%, while Marvell Technology (MRVL) and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) each climbed around 4% on Thursday.

Update: The story was updated with Jefferies' note and stock price movement.

data.title: Fed rate decisions likely to stay data-driven under Warsh - SoFi
data.description:
Market expectations for Federal Reserve rate decisions are unlikely to change dramatically even if Kevin Warsh is confirmed as the central bank’s next chair, according to a new analysis from SoFi released Thursday.

“In reality, the FOMC won’t make rate decisions without substantial amounts of data support, and members will debate the decision based on the facts at hand and the models they rely on,” wrote Liz Thomas, head of Investment Strategy at SoFi.

The assessment comes as the Senate held its confirmation hearing for Warsh, President Donald Trump’s nominee to succeed Jerome Powell, whose term ends May 16. While some observers have questioned whether Warsh would be influenced by political pressure to lower borrowing costs, Thomas noted that the Fed chair is only one of 12 Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) votes that determine rate changes.

“Although they’re tasked with helping build consensus around that decision, they don’t ultimately make the call alone,” Thomas wrote.

Market expectations volatile amid Iran war

Rate expectations have swung wildly in recent months, driven largely by inflation data and uncertainty surrounding the ongoing conflict with Iran. Before the war, markets had anticipated two or three rate cuts in 2026. At one point, expectations swung to the opposite extreme, with markets pricing in the possibility of rate hikes.

Now, markets are pricing in less than a 50% chance of a single cut this year, Thomas noted.

“I don’t expect that to materially change simply because Warsh is head of the Fed, especially since there is still the possibility of an inflationary oil shock,” Thomas wrote.

Balance sheet reduction expected to be Warsh’s focus

The widely held expectation is that Warsh will favor lower rates while also pushing to shrink the Fed’s balance sheet—moves that have somewhat offsetting effects on monetary conditions. The Fed’s balance sheet currently sits at $6.7T, and while Warsh has not indicated a target size, he has said he wants it smaller.

Shrinking the balance sheet would require the private sector to absorb assets the Fed no longer holds, likely necessitating relaxed bank regulations—another area where Warsh is expected to focus.

“Warsh is more likely to drive change through bank deregulation, balance sheet size, and perhaps a shift in the Fed’s approach to communication,” Thomas wrote. “Some of those changes will probably be welcomed by markets, but may take time to materialize.”

data.title: Lockheed stock down after Q1 miss putting spotlight on ETFs with the biggest exposure
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) was down 4% in early trading on Thursday after the largest U.S. defense contractor and maker of fighter jets, missile systems, and military spacecraft reported dour results, missing Wall Street expectations for both revenue and earnings.

Revenue was essentially flat at $17.96B in the first quarter,  below analysts’ consensus estimate of $18.25B, as growth in Missiles and Fire Control and Space helped offset weaker sales in Aeronautics and Rotary and Mission Systems. Adjusted earnings came in at $6.44 a share, below Wall Street expectations, pressured by unfavorable profit adjustments on the F-16 and C-130 programs as well as weaker margins in the Space unit. Net profit also declined from a year earlier as operating performance softened across key segments.

Lockheed Martin reaffirmed its full-year 2026 outlook, maintaining sales guidance of $77.5B to $80B and adjusted earnings per share of $29.35 to $30.25, while keeping free cash flow guidance at $6.5B to $6.8B.

The share price decline has shifted investor focus to ETFs with significant exposure to the defense major, with 24 funds listing the stock among their top 15 holdings.

data.title: The NFL Draft gives prediction markets another event to create waves
data.description:
The NFL Draft is scheduled to take place in Pittsburgh on Thursday night and run through the weekend. The high-profile fan event will give both sports betting apps and prediction markets a fresh burst of business. Sportsbooks should see a seasonal bump from draft props and first-round markets, while prediction markets are emerging as a broader, more accessible alternative that can capture draft interest in states where traditional betting is limited.

Notably, both Kalshi (KALSHI) and Polymarket (POLYMARKET) are offering significantly more NFL draft contracts than a year ago. Kalshi's (KALSHI) draft page shows draft markets split across multiple categories, including draft category, matchups, and over/under formats, while Polymarket (POLYMARKET) lists 159 live draft markets and 20 live NFL draft markets.

Analysts have noted that the NFL Draft sets up similarly to the recent Masters golf tournament, which saw unexpectedly heavy trading volume due to the changing leaderboard. The expectation is the player and pick trades could create the same dynamic that steers volume toward prediction markets. 

Sports betting volume saw a slump in February, but a strong rebound in March tied to increased interest in the NCAA basketball tournament. Following an uneven year for FanDuel (FLUT), DraftKings (DKNG), and BetMGM (MGM) (GMVHF), the World Cup in June and July is anticipated to give the industry a major boost against a soft comparable to a year ago.

Of note, the Roundhill Sports Betting & iGaming ETF (BETZ) is down 10% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Sox crosses 10,000 for the first time as chip rally broadens
data.description:
The PHLX Semiconductor Index surged past the 10,000 mark for the first time on record, marking a symbolic milestone for a sector that has become the backbone of the global equity rally.

The move reflects a powerful combination of AI-driven demand, improving memory cycles, and a rebound in semiconductor capital expenditure. 

According to TradingView data, the index gained over 1.8% on the session, extending a multi-year bull run that has consistently outpaced broader benchmarks.

Crucially, this is not a narrow rally. Gains have been distributed across the semiconductor value chain, from chip designers to foundries and equipment makers, highlighting the depth of the current upcycle.

Earnings revisions have trended higher, particularly in AI-linked segments, while supply-side constraints continue to support pricing power in advanced nodes and high-bandwidth memory.

The index composition underscores that breadth:

  • NVIDIA (NVDA) — $4.91T
  • Broadcom (AVGO) — $2.02T
  • Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) — $1.69T
  • ASML (ASML) — $558.86B
  • Micron Technology (MU) — $541.27B
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) — $498.79B
  • Intel (INTC) — $337.39B
  • Lam Research (LRCX) — $331.24B
  • Applied Materials (AMAT) — $327.01B
  • Texas Instruments (TXN) — $251.33B

While the breakout reinforces semiconductors as the market’s structural growth engine, valuations remain elevated, leaving the index increasingly sensitive to any moderation in AI spending or global demand.

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders approve Paramount Skydance merger
data.description:
• Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) shareholders approved the merger with Paramount Skydance (PSKY) at the special stockholders meeting Thursday morning.
  • "With Paramount, we look forward to creating an exceptional combined company that will expand consumer choice and benefit the global creative talent community,” said Warner Bros. board chairman Samuel Piazza.
  • Paramount Skydance (PSKY) will pay $110.9B for the entire company—or ~$31 per share—to acquire all of its assets, including its movie studio, CNN, HBO, and the company's content library
  • The compensation package to Warner Bros. CEO David Zaslav was not disclosed.
  • The transaction is expected to close in the third quarter of this year, subject to customer closing conditions.
